data.title: Modal & Second Great Quintet
data.article:
After *Kind of Blue* established modal jazz, Miles spent the early 1960s in a period of consolidation and collaboration with Gil Evans. *Sketches of Spain* and *Quiet Nights* extended his orchestral work while the quartet recordings for Columbia continued to evolve.

The Second Great Quintet crystallised around 1964: Miles, Wayne Shorter, Herbie Hancock, Ron Carter, Tony Williams. This group — with an average age of 23 — pushed time, harmony, and interaction to their limits. Their collective improvisation, where the rhythm section was as free as the soloists, influenced every jazz musician who followed.

The studio albums *E.S.P.* (1965), *Miles Smiles* (1966), *Nefertiti* (1967), and *Filles de Kilimanjaro* (1968) documented this evolution. Live recordings from the Plugged Nickel in Chicago (1965) captured the band at their most daring.

By 1968, Miles was listening to Jimi Hendrix, James Brown, and Sly Stone. The quintet was dissolving into something new.
